input.content:
# ApexShot Repository Information

ApexShot is a premium screen capture tool for Linux, featuring a modern landing page and a powerful Rust-based backend.

## Project Structure

- **`frontend/`**: The web landing page for ApexShot.
- **`backend/`**: Contains the core application logic and subprojects.
    - **`backend/`**: The main Rust implementation of the screen capture tool.
    - **`ksnip/`**: A C++ based tool (likely integrated for editing features).
    - **`test_gtk/`**: A small Rust project for testing GTK integration.

---

## 1. Frontend

A beautiful, modern landing page built with the latest web technologies.

- **Tech Stack**:
    - **Framework**: [Next.js 16 (App Router)](https://nextjs.org/)
    - **Library**: [React 19](https://react.dev/)
    - **Styling**: [Tailwind CSS 4](https://tailwindcss.com/), [Framer Motion](https://www.framer.com/motion/)
    - **Graphics**: [Three.js](https://threejs.org/) (via `@react-three/fiber`), [ShaderGradient](https://www.shadergradient.co/)
    - **Language**: [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)
- **Key Features**:
    - Mesh Gradient Backgrounds
    - Mouse-following Spotlight effects
    - Infinite scrolling Marquees
    - Fully Responsive design

---

## 2. Backend (Rust)

The core engine of ApexShot, handling screen capture, OCR, and system integration on Linux.

- **Tech Stack**:
    - **Language**: [Rust](https://www.rust-lang.org/)
    - **Runtime**: [Tokio](https://tokio.rs/) (Async)
    - **GUI/Overlay**: [GTK4](https://www.gtk.org/), [gtk4-layer-shell](https://github.com/wmww/gtk4-layer-shell)
    - **Display Servers**: [X11 (x11rb)](https://github.com/logical-robot/x11rb), [Wayland (wayland-client, ashpd)](https://github.com/fedora-selinux/ashpd)
    - **Media Processing**: [GStreamer](https://gstreamer.freedesktop.org/)
    - **OCR**: [Tesseract](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract)
    - **Clipboard**: [arboard](https://github.com/1Password/arboard)
- **Sub-components**:
    - **Capture Overlay**: C++ based overlay (`backend/backend/capture-overlay/`).
    - **Web Scroll Extension**: Browser extension for scrolling captures (`backend/backend/web-scroll-extension/`).

---

## 3. Ksnip (C++)

An integrated tool for screenshot editing and additional functionality.

- **Tech Stack**:
    - **Language**: C++
    - **Framework**: [Qt](https://www.qt.io/)
    - **Build System**: CMake

---

## 4. Design & Documentation

- **[AGENT.md](./AGENT.md)**: Agent-specific instructions and context.
- **[ApexShot Landing Page Design Document (1).md](./ApexShot%20Landing%20Page%20Design%20Document%20(1).md)**: Detailed design specifications for the landing page.
- **[setup-frontend.sh](./setup-frontend.sh)**: Script to automate frontend environment setup.

---

## Getting Started

### Frontend
```bash
cd frontend
pnpm install
pnpm dev
```

### Backend
```bash
cd backend/backend
cargo build
```

content.text:
## Fixed

I restored the **GNOME Wayland preference for ScreenCast/screensharing** before the screenshot portal fallback.

- **[./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:92](./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:92)**: added GNOME Wayland detection.
- **[./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:431](./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:431)**: extracted the ScreenCast monitor capture path into a reusable helper.
- **[./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:451](./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:451)**: changed `capture_screen_chain()` so GNOME Wayland now tries **ScreenCast first**, then only falls back to the Screenshot portal if ScreenCast fails.
- **[./backend/src/main.rs:679](./backend/src/main.rs:679)**: updated the startup log so it no longer claims the flash/sound path is the expected default.

## Verified

- **`cargo check`**: passed
- **`cargo test`**: passed

## Next

Please **restart the daemon/app** and try area capture again on GNOME Wayland. You should now see logs preferring ScreenCast before the screenshot portal. If you want, I can also help you verify the runtime logs after you test it.

## Yes — this looks **fixable**

From the code, the most likely problem is **not PNG/JPEG compression**.  
It looks more like a **Wayland/HiDPI scaling mismatch** during the **area capture fallback**.

## Why I think that

- **PNG saves are lossless** in the main path: [./backend/src/capture/mod.rs:406](./backend/src/capture/mod.rs:406)
- So if the image already looks soft in PNG, the damage is happening **before save**.

The strongest suspect is here:

- [./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:581](./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:581)  
  `capture_area_direct_impl()` captures the full screen, then crops it.
- [./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:200](./backend/src/backend/wayland.rs:200)  
  `crop_capture()` uses the incoming `x, y, width, height` **directly**.

That means if your GNOME Wayland session is running at **125% / 150% / 200% scaling**, the selection rectangle is probably in **logical coordinates**, but the captured image may be in **physical pixels**.  
That would produce an image that is effectively **too small**, which then looks blurry.

## Why this is especially suspicious

Your earlier log showed:

- captured result width/height around **`600x744`**

That matches the app’s default logical selection size much more than a full-resolution physical crop on a scaled display.

Also, the C++ side already has code that accounts for this kind of scaling:

- [./backend/capture-overlay/src/ScreenCapture.cpp:799](./backend/capture-overlay/src/ScreenCapture.cpp:799)

It computes:

- `scaleX = fullImage.width() / desktopBounds.width()`
- `scaleY = fullImage.height() / desktopBounds.height()`

But the Rust Wayland fallback path does **not** do equivalent scaling before cropping.

## So the likely answer is

- **Yes, we can probably improve quality**
- The first thing to fix is:
  1. detect the scale ratio between the captured full-screen image and the logical desktop bounds
  2. scale the selected area before cropping in the Rust Wayland fallback path

## Less likely, but secondary possibilities

If **fullscreen captures** are also blurry, then we should also inspect:

- the **ScreenCast/PipeWire stream resolution**
- whether GNOME is returning a lower-resolution stream in that path
- any Qt fallback path like [./backend/capture-overlay/src/ScreenCapture.cpp:309](./backend/capture-overlay/src/ScreenCapture.cpp:309)

But based on the code, **the area crop scaling bug is the first thing I’d fix**.

content.text:
## Patched

I ended up fixing this in the **C++ capture layer**, not the UI itself.

### What changed
- **[./backend/capture-overlay/src/main.cpp:287](./backend/capture-overlay/src/main.cpp:287)**
  - GNOME Wayland area capture now passes the **overlay geometry** instead of only the overlay size.
- **[./backend/capture-overlay/src/ScreenCapture.h:21](./backend/capture-overlay/src/ScreenCapture.h:21)**
  - Updated the helper signature accordingly.
- **[./backend/capture-overlay/src/ScreenCapture.cpp:863](./backend/capture-overlay/src/ScreenCapture.cpp:863)**
  - Reworked `captureAreaToTempPngFromOverlayLocal(...)` so it maps the selected rect using:
    - the **actual overlay geometry**
    - the **logical desktop bounds**
    - separate **X/Y scaling**
  - This replaces the old GNOME-specific **bottom-alignment guess**, which was more likely to mis-map crops on scaled displays.

## Why this is a better target
The GNOME Wayland `--area-init` path was already staying in the **C++ capture path**, so this fix keeps your preferred split intact:
- **C++**: capture/selector behavior
- **Rust**: backend/editor/settings

## Verified
- **`cargo check`**: passed
- **`cargo test`**: passed
- **C++ capture overlay build**: passed

## Next
Please try another **area capture on GNOME Wayland** and compare the sharpness. If it still looks soft, the next thing I’d inspect is the **fullscreen source image resolution** coming from the ScreenCast/GNOME capture path.
